phases.label: Per-task craft
phases.blurb: Framing each task and proving it's done — the work-specific arc.

phases.label: Session-wide habits
phases.blurb: Discipline that runs across the whole session, not one task.

tiers.label: Adequate
tiers.help: Meets the baseline for this dimension.

tiers.label: Developing
tiers.help: Evidence is present but falls short — the highest-leverage place to improve.

tiers.label: Not enough signal
tiers.help: This behavior didn't occur or produced too little signal to grade. Not a weakness.

tiers.label: Not capturable
tiers.help: This agent can't emit the telemetry for this dimension (e.g. no token signal on Cursor). Not a reflection of the engineer.

dimensions.label: Direction
dimensions.measures: Whether the engineer set the agent up to succeed — specific asks, the context only they have, and work shaped so each step could be done right.
dimensions.detail: Direction is everything the engineer controls before and while the agent works: how precisely each ask is stated, how much of the context only they possess gets handed over, and how the work is shaped so every step has what it needs. The highest-value part is latent context — the intent, scale assumptions, and non-goals the agent cannot discover from the repo ("we'll only ever have one user"). Withholding it doesn't cost turns; it produces confidently-wrong output. Structuring is the counterweight to naive decomposition: over-chop the work and each step is locally fine but globally wrong because no unit saw the whole — plan-then-execute (plan mode where it earns it) is how you decompose without starving context. Judged from prompt content and work shape, not from raw turn count, which conflates prompt quality with task difficulty.

anchors.developing: Vague asks with the context left in the engineer's head; work sprawls or fragments.
anchors.adequate: Clear goal and rough shape, but key intent, constraints, or ordering stay implicit.
anchors.strong: Specific asks carrying the context only they have, shaped so each step can be done right.

subFacets.label: Specificity
subFacets.signal: What each prompt pins down — expected-vs-actual behavior, constraints, acceptance criteria, pasted evidence — versus what's left to the model to guess.

anchors.developing: Vague asks ('fix it') — no target behavior, no constraints, no acceptance bar.
anchors.adequate: Names the goal and some constraints, but leaves room for the agent to guess.
anchors.strong: Pins expected behavior, constraints, and the acceptance check in a single ask.

subFacets.label: Latent context
subFacets.signal: Whether the engineer supplied context the agent can't discover from the repo — intent, scale assumptions, non-goals, what's throwaway versus load-bearing. Inferred from what was stated up front versus assumptions the agent had to make.

anchors.developing: Withholds the constraints only they know; the agent builds confidently for the wrong world.
anchors.adequate: Gives task context but leaves key intent or scale assumptions unsaid.
anchors.strong: States the intent, scale, and non-goals up front so the agent optimizes for the real target.

subFacets.label: Structuring
subFacets.signal: Whether the work was shaped so each step had the context to be right — plan-then-execute versus one mega-prompt versus blind over-chopping. Plan-mode usage that produced coherent scoped steps is read here.

anchors.developing: One sprawling ask across many concerns, or blind steps that don't cohere on integration.
anchors.adequate: Breaks the work up, but some steps miss the big picture or overlap.
anchors.strong: Plans the whole, then executes in scoped steps — each with the context it needs.

sources.name: Anthropic
sources.detail: Claude Code best practices, AI Fluency framework, plan-mode guidance

sources.name: Mitchell Hashimoto
sources.detail: Plan-first prompting, never ship unreviewed AI code

dimensions.label: Verification
dimensions.measures: Whether the output was actually exercised before it was trusted — runs, tests, and hands-on checks, sized to the risk of the change.
dimensions.detail: The flagship dimension, and the most reliably observable: the strong signal is that something actually ran the generated code before the engineer trusted it. Because the agent runs commands through its own tools, every test, build, dev-server, and `!`-prefixed run is in the transcript — the main path is fully captured. The blind spot is out-of-band verification (running tests in a separate terminal, checking in an editor): where there's genuinely no observed signal, this scores insufficient-evidence, never failing — absence of observed verification is not proof it didn't happen. Verification is sized to risk: edge and failure-path tests on logic that needs them, not the full CI suite on a rename. Over-verifying trivial changes is a Context (cost) failure; under-verifying risky ones is the failure here.

anchors.developing: Trusts output without exercising it; a green-looking result is treated as proof.
anchors.adequate: Runs the code, but after the fact or without matching the effort to the risk.
anchors.strong: Exercises the change as it goes, with verification sized to the blast radius.

subFacets.label: Exercised the output
subFacets.signal: Whether something actually ran the generated code — tests, a build, a dev server, a command (including `!`-prefixed runs) — before it was trusted.

anchors.developing: Ships edits without running anything; a green-looking result is taken as proof.
anchors.adequate: Runs the code, but only after the fact or only the happy path.
anchors.strong: Exercises the change as it goes — the output is seen running before it's trusted.

subFacets.label: Proportionate testing
subFacets.signal: Whether verification is sized to the risk — edge, boundary, and failure-path tests on logic that needs them, without burning the full suite on a trivial change.

anchors.developing: Either no tests where the change is risky, or reflexive full-suite runs on trivial edits.
anchors.adequate: Tests the change, but coverage doesn't track the risk — thin where it's dangerous, heavy where it's not.
anchors.strong: Verification matches the blast radius — boundary and failure-path tests where it counts, light touch where it doesn't.

subFacets.label: Manual verification
subFacets.signal: Text tells that the human checked the running result themselves — 'clicking this does nothing', 'the layout's off'. Adds evidence when present; never penalized when absent (absence floors at insufficient-evidence, not developing).

anchors.developing: No sign either way — scored as no-signal, not a weakness.
anchors.adequate: A passing mention of a manual check ('looks right').
anchors.strong: Concrete manual verification narrated — a specific observed result, 'clicked through, the error's gone'.

sources.name: Simon Willison
sources.detail: “If you haven’t seen it run, it’s not a working system”

sources.name: DORA 2025
sources.detail: AI amplifies only with verification and control systems

dimensions.label: Context
dimensions.measures: Whether the engineer kept the working context lean and configured — resetting at boundaries, not bloating what's loaded every turn, and keeping token-saving setup turned on.
dimensions.detail: Context is the controllable process behind cost. Most raw token burn is the model's behavior — it decides which files to re-read, and redundant reads balloon as the window fills — so we don't score the engineer on a number they didn't drive; that's the Cost band, reported separately. What the engineer does control: resetting or compacting at task boundaries instead of running ten tasks in one bloated window; keeping the always-loaded setup lean (an oversized CLAUDE.md, a stack of plugins, or a memory file that taxes every single turn — measured by cc-audit); and whether the token-saving setup is even switched on. Model right-sizing is surfaced here as a beta signal while we calibrate it — it does not yet affect the score.

anchors.developing: Bloated, unmanaged context and efficiency features left off — every turn pays the tax.
anchors.adequate: Workable context and partial setup, but with dead weight and incidental resets.
anchors.strong: Lean context, deliberate resets, and a setup configured to save tokens turn over turn.

subFacets.label: Context hygiene
subFacets.signal: Whether context is reset or compacted at task boundaries versus one bloated window carrying ten tasks of stale state.

anchors.developing: Runs task after task in one window until quality rots — or panic-clears and re-pastes.
anchors.adequate: Keeps context workable, but resets are incidental, not deliberate.
anchors.strong: Compacts at natural boundaries on purpose — reset as a steering tool, not a reflex.

subFacets.label: Asset bloat
subFacets.signal: Whether the always-loaded setup is lean — CLAUDE.md, skills, memory, and plugins that don't burn context every turn (measured by cc-audit).

anchors.developing: Bloated always-on context — an oversized CLAUDE.md or stack of plugins taxes every turn.
anchors.adequate: Setup is functional but carries dead weight that could be trimmed.
anchors.strong: Lean, high-signal setup — every always-loaded token earns its place.

subFacets.label: Config present & on
subFacets.signal: Whether the token-saving setup is even turned on — auto-memory enabled, CLAUDE.md present at both user and project level.

anchors.developing: Efficiency features off — no memory, no CLAUDE.md, re-explaining the same context every session.
anchors.adequate: Some setup in place, but gaps force repeated re-explanation.
anchors.strong: The environment is configured to remember — memory on, project + user CLAUDE.md carrying the standing context.

subFacets.label: Model right-sizing
subFacets.signal: Whether model choice tracks task weight — not the heaviest model on trivial edits. Surfaced while we calibrate; does not affect the score yet.

anchors.developing: Heaviest model on everything, including renames and one-line edits.
anchors.adequate: Model choice is mostly a default, occasionally matched to the task.
anchors.strong: Model tracks task weight — heavy where it earns it, light where it doesn't.

sources.name: Peter Steinberger
sources.detail: Blast-radius task sizing, context-tax awareness

sources.name: Thorsten Ball
sources.detail: Analyze-then-commit prompting, fresh-thread discipline

dimensions.label: Leverage
dimensions.measures: Whether the engineer operates the AI-coding ecosystem well — the right tool for the job, durable workflows codified, and parallel execution where it moves faster.
dimensions.detail: Leverage is how well the engineer operates the ecosystem around the agent, across two clusters. Workflow: wiring up and using the right tool for the job — skills, hooks (format-to-standard), scoped permissions, MCP servers for db and auth — and not reaching for a token-hungry dynamic workflow where a one-liner would do. Its top marker is workflow codification: converting repeated knowledge into the right durable artifact (a recurring model mistake becomes a repo instruction, a missed edge case a regression test, a mechanical operation a script, a dangerous behavior a permission guardrail) and removing obsolete ones — the closed feedback loop that's the clearest sign of an advanced operator. Velocity: running independent work in parallel where it helps — worktrees for isolation, concurrent sessions, work partitioned so streams don't collide. Because quality is scored separately in the bands, rewarding parallel speed here can't reward slop. This dimension is heavily tool-specific: engineers are credited for using what's available and never penalized for tools their environment doesn't offer.

anchors.developing: Works serially by hand, ignoring the tooling and durable workflows that would help.
anchors.adequate: Uses parts of the ecosystem with mixed payoff; little gets codified for next time.
anchors.strong: Right tool for each job, repeated work codified into durable assets, independent streams run in parallel.

subFacets.label: Tooling
subFacets.signal: Whether the ecosystem is wired up and used where it pays off — skills, hooks, scoped permissions, MCP servers for db/auth — and the right-sized tool for the job (no million-token workflow for a one-liner).

anchors.developing: Ignores leverage that would help, or fires heavyweight tooling where a simple call would do.
anchors.adequate: Uses the ecosystem occasionally, with mixed payoff.
anchors.strong: Right tool at the right moment — hooks, permissions, MCP, and skills each close a real loop; no tool theater.

subFacets.label: Workflow codification
subFacets.signal: Whether repeated knowledge is converted into the right durable artifact — skill vs script vs test vs lint rule vs CLAUDE.md vs CI — with obsolete ones removed. The closed failure→guardrail loop.

anchors.developing: Re-types the same workflows every time; nothing gets captured for next time.
anchors.adequate: Captures some repetition, but into the wrong form (a skill for what should be CI) or never prunes it.
anchors.strong: Recurring work becomes the right durable mechanism — a repeated mistake becomes a rule, a missed edge case a test — and stale ones are removed.

subFacets.label: Velocity
subFacets.signal: Whether independent work is run in parallel where it helps — worktrees for isolation, concurrent sessions, work partitioned so streams don't collide. (Cross-session signal needs fleet correlation.)

anchors.developing: Everything serial, even independent workstreams that could run at once.
anchors.adequate: Occasionally parallelizes, but without isolation — streams step on each other.
anchors.strong: Independent work runs concurrently in isolated worktrees/sessions, partitioned to land cleanly.

sources.name: Anthropic
sources.detail: Claude Code best practices, subagent + skills guidance

sources.name: Armin Ronacher
sources.detail: Oracle-before-autonomy, lean tooling over MCP sprawl

methodologySummary: Graded against agentic-coding standards published by Anthropic and OpenAI, the documented workflows of practitioners like Boris Cherny (creator of Claude Code), Andrej Karpathy, and Simon Willison, and measurement research from METR, DORA, and GitClear. Four process dimensions score how the engineer works with the agent — a predictor of code quality, kept separate from delivery outcomes so the correlation stays real. Each carries a reliability tier: how confidently the behavior can be judged from session telemetry alone. Every dimension is scored by three independent judge passes aggregated by lower median — a split jury never rounds up, and any dissent caps confidence.
